column4: MORSE is built on the Blender 3D engine and uses Blender's Python scripting environment. Unique among simulators for its middleware-agnostic design: supports ROS, YARP, Pocolibs, HLA, and socket-based communication simultaneously. No longer maintained as of ~2020; requires Blender 2.79b (outdated). MORSE was the primary academic robotics simulator in Europe before Gazebo gained dominance. 4,356 total commits over its active life.

column4: Installation is challenging due to strict Blender 2.79b dependency (discontinued). Modern Ubuntu repositories provide a newer Blender version that is incompatible. Requires manually downloading and installing the legacy Blender 2.79b binary. This is the most significant barrier. The build process itself is straightforward once Blender 2.79b is in place.

column4: Project is unmaintained since ~2020. No new releases since MORSE 1.4 (2016). The fundamental dependency on Blender 2.79b (itself end-of-life in 2018) means MORSE cannot easily be updated. Low maintainability score reflects current state rather than historical quality.

column4: Unique middleware-agnostic architecture allows connecting to ROS, YARP, and Pocolibs simultaneously — a design feature not found in most simulators. Component-based architecture (sensors, actuators, robots as composable objects) is highly reusable. However, the Blender 2.79b dependency severely limits current practical reusability.
